summary: My name is Alfred Charles Anderson, but nobody calls me Alfred. To my family, I'm Al, and in the military, they call me Andy or chief. I was born in Philadelphia but grew up mostly around Wilmington and later Viola, Delaware. My experiences from childhood through my military service shaped who I am today.

I was born in Philadelphia, though I don't often mention it. Later, after induction into the Army, I traveled back through Philadelphia on my way to Camden, New Jersey. Philadelphia was part of my early life and military journey.

I consider Wilmington my primary home since I lived there from age 5 to 14. It was a big part of my childhood before my family moved to Viola, Delaware. I also had connections with other boys from Wilmington when I joined the military.

When I was 14, my family moved to Viola, Delaware, a tiny town with only 105 people. Life there was simple, with a pump on the porch and a cook stove heating the house. I vividly remember being at home in Viola on Pearl Harbor Day in 1941, feeling stunned and alarmed with my brothers.

I remember Pearl Harbor Day very clearly. It was December 7th, 1941, and I was at home in Viola with my brothers. We learned about the attack in the early afternoon and stayed glued to the radio all evening, shocked and alarmed by the news.

After Pearl Harbor, I tried to join the Navy but was rejected because I was colorblind. They had earlier tried to recruit me, but I declined, deciding to wait for the draft instead. The Navy missed their chance with me, and I ended up joining the Army.

I was drafted into the United States Army on February 19th, 1943, after some delay and personal rebellion. I left from Harrington, Delaware, traveled through Philadelphia and Camden, and eventually trained at Fort Dix. I served in various roles and locations throughout the war.

I left for the Army from Harrington, Delaware. From there, I traveled to Philadelphia and Camden before heading south for training. Harrington was the last place I was before entering active military service.

Philadelphia was both my birthplace and a key transit point during my military induction. After leaving Harrington, I went to Philadelphia before crossing over to Camden, New Jersey. It was part of the journey that started my Army career.

After Philadelphia, I crossed the river to Camden, New Jersey, where I was escorted by police because some Delaware boys had caused trouble the day before. Camden was a notable stop early in my Army induction process.

When I was inducted, both the Marine Corps and Navy tried to recruit me again, but I refused. I told them they had missed their chance and that I was going to serve in the Army. I was determined to follow my own path in the military.

I spent a few days at Fort Dix after induction before shipping out. It was one of my first stops in the Army, and from there, I began my journey south for training. Fort Dix marked the start of my active military training.

I trained for 18 days in Miami Beach, Florida, which was a lively and jubilant time despite the brief duration. I was 18 years old and surrounded by tough guys from North Jersey and New York City. Miami Beach was where I first experienced basic training.

Many of the men I trained with in Miami Beach were from North Jersey. They were a tough bunch and part of the diverse group I met early in my military career. Their presence made the training environment intense and memorable.

Along with men from North Jersey, many of my fellow trainees in Miami Beach came from New York City. They were a tough crowd, and together we formed a lively group during basic training. New York City was part of the mix of backgrounds I encountered.

I volunteered to be an aerial gunner and was sent to Buckley Field, Colorado, for armament school and gunnery training. This was where I earned my sergeant rank, leather jacket, goggles, and wings. Buckley Field was a pivotal place in my military career.

After Buckley Field, I also trained in Denver, Colorado, as part of my aerial gunnery schooling. Denver was part of the process that prepared me for combat missions as an aerial gunner. These locations shaped my skills and rank advancement.

I was assigned to Moses Lake, Washington, for further training after Salt Lake City. I didn't realize Washington had desert areas until I arrived there, living in tar paper shacks surrounded by black sand and rocks. Moses Lake was a tough but important training site.

Due to bad weather in Moses Lake, I was transferred to Drew Field in Tampa, Florida. I traveled there by train and trained alongside other airmen. Drew Field was where I continued my preparation for aerial missions.

I trained in Tampa, Florida, at Drew Field and stayed there before moving on to Langley Field. It was the only time I shared sleeping quarters with another man in the military due to the Pullman bunks. Tampa was a key stop in my training journey.

At Langley Field, Virginia, I received radio training and flew with the 4th Sea Search unit. We flew up and down the coast for two months, and it was the first time I came home in ten months. Langley Field was where I gained important skills and flew many training missions.

I was assigned to the 4th Sea Search unit at Langley Field, flying coastal patrols for two months. This unit flew missions up and down the Atlantic coast, and it was during this time I first returned home after many months away. The 4th Sea Search was an important part of my early combat experience.

During my training flights from Langley Field, we flew over Harrisburg, Pennsylvania, among other cities. These cross-country flights helped me study the coastline and prepare for combat. Harrisburg was one of the familiar landmarks on these missions.

Pittsburgh was another city we flew over during cross-country training missions from Langley Field. Flying over Pittsburgh gave me a sense of connection to home and the country as I prepared for combat. These flights were both educational and nostalgic.

After Langley Field, I was shipped out to Grenier Airport in New Hampshire. This was part of my deployment process before heading further north to Goose Bay, Labrador. Grenier Airport was a stepping stone in my overseas assignments.

I was stationed briefly in New Hampshire at Grenier Airport before moving on to other locations. New Hampshire was part of my route to the northern bases where I flew missions. It was a cold, transitional place in my military journey.

I was assigned to Goose Bay in Labrador, where snow piled 12 to 14 feet high on either side of the aircraft. The conditions were harsh and challenging, but it was a critical base for our operations. Goose Bay was one of the most remote and cold places I served.

My time in Labrador was spent at Goose Bay, where the snow was incredibly deep and the environment tough. It was a remote northern location that tested our endurance and flying skills. Labrador was a key part of the northern air route during the war.

I flew individual flights to Meeks Field in Iceland, where I once got lost due to bad weather and nearly ran out of fuel. It was a tense experience that tested my skills and nerves. Meeks Field was a vital stopover in the North Atlantic air route.

Iceland was part of my flight route, specifically Meeks Field, where I encountered severe weather and navigational challenges. The experience was intense, including a night spent there after nearly running out of fuel. Iceland was a critical but difficult location on my missions.

After Iceland, I flew into Nutts Corner Airport in Ireland. This was part of the journey before splitting up to Bovingdon Airfield for further training and lectures. Nutts Corner was an important waypoint in my European deployment.

I landed at Nutts Corner Airport in Ireland during my deployment to Europe. Ireland was a staging area before moving on to Bovingdon Airfield in England. It was part of the transition from North Atlantic flights to active combat zones.

At Bovingdon Airfield in England, we received lectures from experienced airmen and British bomber crews. It was a sobering experience, hearing about the dangers and the high casualty rates. Bovingdon was where I learned the harsh realities of air combat.

I flew with the Eighth Air Force over Europe, where the missions were dangerous and conditions brutal. Early crews suffered heavy losses without fighter escort, but by the time I flew, we were beginning to get escorts. The Eighth Air Force was central to the air war in Europe.

I saw one of the first Messerschmitt Me 262 jets come through our formation. It was so fast that pilots were stunned and unsure what they were seeing. Facing these new German jets was terrifying and added to the danger of our missions.
